Older followers of Christ must behave in every way according to Christian teaching. Older men should be sensible; not thinking that the decline of their bodies will excuse any excess, but seeking comfort from closer fellowship with God, not from any improper indulgence. Faith works by love, and it must be seen in love for God himself, and of people for God’s sake. Older people are often irritable; therefore they need to be on guard. Although there is no specific Scripture for every word or look, there are general rules according to which all things must be ordered. Young women should be sensible and discreet; because many expose themselves to serious temptations by what at first might only be a lack of discretion. This reason is added so that the Word of God will not be blasphemed. Failures in duties greatly dishonor Christianity. Young men are often eager and thoughtless, therefore they must be earnestly called upon to be sensible: there are more young people ruined by pride than by any other sin. Every godly person’s effort should be to stop the mouths of opponents. Let your own conscience answer for your honesty. What a glory it is for a Christian when that mouth which would fain open itself against him cannot find any evil in him to speak of!

Servants must know and do their duty to their earthly masters, with reference to their heavenly one. In serving an earthly master according to Christ’s will, He is served; such people will be rewarded by Him. They should not use disrespectful or provoking language, but take a check or reproof silently, not making confident or bold replies. When conscious of a fault, excusing or justifying it doubles it. They should never put something to their own use that belongs to their master, nor waste the goods they are entrusted with. Showing all good faithfulness will improve a master’s goods and promote his success. If you have not been faithful in that which is another man's, who will give you that which is your own? True religion is an honor to those who profess it; and they should adorn it in all things.

The teaching of grace and salvation by the gospel is for all ranks and conditions of people. It teaches them to forsake sin and have no more to do with it. An earthly, sensual lifestyle does not suit a heavenly calling. It teaches them to be careful about what is good. We must look to God in Christ as the object of our hope and worship. A gospel lifestyle must be a godly lifestyle. See our duty in a few words: denying ungodliness and worldly desires, living soberly, righteously, and godly, despite all snares, temptations, corrupt examples, mistreatment, and what remains of sin in the believer’s heart, with all their hindrances. It teaches us to look for the glories of another world. At and in the glorious appearing of Christ, the blessed hope of Christians will be complete: To bring us to holiness and happiness was the purpose of Christ's death. Jesus Christ, that great God and our Savior, saves not only as God, much less as Man alone; but as God-man, two natures in one person. He loved us and gave Himself for us; and what can we do less than love and give ourselves to Him! Redemption from sin and purification of nature go together, and make a special people unto God, free from guilt and condemnation, and purified by the Holy Spirit. All Scripture is useful. Here is what will furnish for all parts of duty, and the right discharge of them. Let us inquire whether our whole dependence is placed upon that grace which saves the lost, pardons the guilty, and sanctifies the unclean. And the further we are removed from boasting of fancied good works, or trusting in them, so that we glory in Christ alone, the more zealous shall we be to abound in real good works.
